content.problemStatement:
You have a garden with NNN flowers planted in a row. The ithi^\text{th}ith flower will bloom on day bloomDay[i]bloomDay[i]bloomDay[i]. Once a flower blooms, it can be used to make a bouquet **exactly once**.

You want to make **m bouquets**, and each bouquet requires **k adjacent flowers** from the garden.

Function DeclarationFunction NameminDaysminDaysminDays – This function calculates the minimum number of days needed to make the required bouquets.

Parameters
bloomDaybloomDaybloomDay : An array where bloomDay[i]bloomDay[i]bloomDay[i] represents the day the ithi^\text{th}ith flower blooms.
mmm : The number of bouquets required.
kkk : The number of **adjacent flowers** needed per bouquet.
Return Value
Returns a single integer — the **minimum number of days** required to make mmm bouquets.
Returns −1-1−1 if it is **impossible** to make the bouquets.
Constraints
1≤N≤1051 ≤ N ≤ 10^51≤N≤105
1≤bloomDay[i]≤1091 ≤ bloomDay[i] ≤ 10^91≤bloomDay[i]≤109
1≤m≤1051 ≤ m ≤ 10^51≤m≤105
1≤k≤N1 ≤ k ≤ N1≤k≤N
Each flower can be used **only once**
Flowers used in a bouquet must be **adjacent**

Input Format


First line contains three integers: NNN, mmm, kkk — the number of flowers, the number of bouquets, and the number of adjacent flowers needed per bouquet.




Second line contains NNN space-separated integers: bloomDay[1],bloomDay[2],...,bloomDay[N]bloomDay[1], bloomDay[2], ..., bloomDay[N]bloomDay[1],bloomDay[2],...,bloomDay[N] — the day each flower blooms.





Output Format

Print a single integer — the minimum number of days required.
Print −1-1−1 if it is impossible to form mmm bouquets.
content.inputFormat: First line contains three integers: N, the number of flowers, m, the number of bouquets, and k, the number of adjacent flowers needed per bouquet. Second line contains N space-separated integers: bloomDay[1], bloomDay[2], ..., bloomDay[N] — the day each flower blooms.
content.outputFormat: Print a single integer — the minimum number of days required to make m bouquets. Print -1 if it is impossible to form m bouquets.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
First case: after day 3 we have three bloomed flowers at positions 1,3,5, enough for 3 bouquets of size 1.
Second case: need 6 flowers for 3 bouquets of size 2 but only 5 exist, impossible.
Third case: after day 6 the first six flowers are bloomed, giving three pairs, enough for 5 bouquets of size 2 (using positions 1-2,3-4,5-6, and the remaining two pairs can be formed from later flowers).
The earliest day when we can obtain two disjoint groups of three consecutive bloomed flowers is day 10, when all flowers have bloomed.
The earliest day when we can obtain one group of five consecutive bloomed flowers is day 5, when all flowers have bloomed.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a greedy algorithm combined with binary search. The idea is to find the minimum number of days required to make m bouquets. We can use binary search to find the minimum number of days. For each possible number of days, we can check if it is possible to make m bouquets by iterating through the bloomDay array and counting the number of bouquets that can be made.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * log(max(bloomDay))) per test case, where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of flowers, and max(bloomDay) is the maximum number of days a flower can bloom.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — for storing the bloomDay array.
